title: $IOPRINT
text: $IOPRINT Specify the types of messages sent to the message frame or log file. $IOPRINT = MessageLevel Arguments MessageLevel —integer representing the sum of the message codes for the desired message types. If the sum of the codes is 0 , no information is placed in the message frame. For more information, see the "I/O Message Levels" topic. Description The $IOPRINT determines the types of messages that will appear in the message frame. You can use the ProcScript function $ioprint to test or change the current I/O message level. This setting only takes effect after the assignment file has been read and overrides the value set by /pri , which is applied immediately upon startup. Example: Setting $IOPRINT [SETTINGS] $IOPRINT = 63 Copy 63 is the sum of all message codes up to and including Generated SQL (if available). Therefore, the message frame returns all messages for codes 1, 2, 4, 8, 16 and 32. Related Links $ioprint Return or set the message level in the message frame. /pri Specify the types of messages sent to the message frame or log file. I/O Message Levels Most of the messages that display in the message frame are determined by the I/O message level. The I/O message is a number that is determined by adding up the codes of the applicable message types. Runtime Messages and Log Files All Uniface applications can be configured to create log files that collect runtime messages generated during the application session. The messages may be generated by ProcScript commands in your application, or by Uniface to document actions, events, warnings and errors. You can configure which Uniface runtime messages are reported. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
